Since 1922, the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") has been made up of four countries: [England](/wiki/England "England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") (which collectively make up [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain")) and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") ([variously described](#Terminology) as a country,[[1]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-1) province,[[2]](#cite_note-ISO-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) jurisdiction[[5]](#cite_note-alphabeticalNI-5) or region[[6]](#cite_note-interpretingNI-6)[[7]](#cite_note-placeApart-7)). The UK [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom")'s website has used the phrase "countries within a country" to describe the United Kingdom.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Although the United Kingdom is a [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state"), it contains three distinct legal jurisdictions in [Scotland](/wiki/Scots_Law "Scots Law"), [England and Wales](/wiki/English_Law "English Law"), and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Irish_Law "Northern Irish Law"), each retaining its own legal system even after joining the UK.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Since 1998, Northern Ireland, Scotland, and Wales have also gained significant autonomy through the process of [devolution](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom"). The [UK Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") and [UK Government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") deal with all [reserved matters](/wiki/Devolved,_reserved_and_excepted_matters "Devolved, reserved and excepted matters") for Northern Ireland, Scotland, and Wales, but not in general matters that have been [devolved](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") to the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly"), [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament"), and [Senedd](/wiki/Senedd "Senedd"). Additionally, devolution in Northern Ireland is conditional on co-operation between the [Northern Ireland Executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive") and the [Government of Ireland](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland "Government of Ireland") (see [North/South Ministerial Council](/wiki/North/South_Ministerial_Council "North/South Ministerial Council")) and the British Government consults with the Government of Ireland to reach agreement on some non-devolved matters for Northern Ireland (see [British–Irish Intergovernmental Conference](/wiki/British%E2%80%93Irish_Intergovernmental_Conference "British–Irish Intergovernmental Conference")). England, comprising the majority of the population and area of the United Kingdom,[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-ONSCOUNTRYPROFILES-11) does not have its own devolved government, and remains fully the responsibility of the United Kingdom Parliament centralised in [London](/wiki/London "London"). In July 2025, [the council](/wiki/Cornwall_Council "Cornwall Council") of the [English county](/wiki/English_county "English county") of [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") backed a motion calling for Cornwall to be regarded as the [UK's fifth nation](/wiki/Constitutional_status_of_Cornwall "Constitutional status of Cornwall").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

England, Northern Ireland, Scotland, and Wales are not themselves listed in the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") (ISO) [list of countries](/wiki/Countries_in_the_International_Organization_for_Standardization "Countries in the International Organization for Standardization"). However, the ISO list of the subdivisions of the United Kingdom, compiled by [British Standards](/wiki/British_Standards "British Standards") and the United Kingdom's [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics"), uses "country" to describe England, Scotland, and Wales.[[14]](#cite_note-ISO_Newsletter_ii-3-2011-12-13-14) Northern Ireland, in contrast, is described as a "province" in the same lists.[[14]](#cite_note-ISO_Newsletter_ii-3-2011-12-13-14) Each has separate [national governing bodies for sports](/wiki/Sport_governing_body "Sport governing body") and compete separately in many international sporting competitions, including the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"). Northern Ireland also forms joint [All-Island](/wiki/Island_of_Ireland "Island of Ireland") sporting bodies with the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") for some sports, including [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands") and the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man") are [dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies") of [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") and are not part of the United Kingdom. Similarly, the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories"), remnants of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), are not part of the UK.

From 1801, following the [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800"), until 1922 the whole island of [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") was a country within the United Kingdom. Ireland was [split into two separate jurisdictions](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920 "Government of Ireland Act 1920") in 1921, becoming [Southern Ireland](/wiki/Southern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%9322) "Southern Ireland (1921–22)") and Northern Ireland. Pursuant to the [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty"), the institutions of the [revolutionary Irish Republic](/wiki/Irish_Republic "Irish Republic") were assimilated into Southern Ireland, which then became the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") and left the United Kingdom in 1922. The Irish Free State adopted a new, essentially republican [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Ireland "Constitution of Ireland") in 1937 – albeit [retaining the King for diplomatic functions](/wiki/Irish_head_of_state_from_1922_to_1949#Ambiguity_from_1936_to_1949 "Irish head of state from 1922 to 1949") – by which it would be known as simply Ireland. In 1949, by [The Republic of Ireland Act 1948](/wiki/The_Republic_of_Ireland_Act_1948 "The Republic of Ireland Act 1948"), it transferred these diplomatic functions to [its own president](/wiki/President_of_Ireland "President of Ireland"), left the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/British_Commonwealth "British Commonwealth") and adopted the description Republic of Ireland, by which it is now known.

* The [Laws in Wales Acts 1535 and 1542](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535_and_1542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535 and 1542") annexed Wales to England[[22]](#cite_note-LWA1535I-28) to create the single legal entity, though legal differences remained. Further Acts meant this combined territory was referred to in law simply as 'England' from [1746](/wiki/Wales_and_Berwick_Act_1746 "Wales and Berwick Act 1746") until [1967](/wiki/Welsh_Language_Act_1967 "Welsh Language Act 1967"). Wales was described (in varying combinations) as the "country", "principality", and "dominion" of Wales.[[22]](#cite_note-LWA1535I-28)[[23]](#cite_note-LWA1542-29) The Laws in Wales Acts have subsequently been repealed.[[24]](#cite_note-30)[[25]](#cite_note-31)
* The [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") refer to both England and Scotland as a "part" of a united [kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain").[[26]](#cite_note-32)
* The [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") use "part" in the same way to refer to England and Scotland. However, they use the word "country" to describe Great Britain and Ireland respectively, when describing trade between them.[[27]](#cite_note-33)
* The [Government of Ireland Act 1920](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920 "Government of Ireland Act 1920") described Great Britain, [Southern Ireland](/wiki/Southern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%9322) "Southern Ireland (1921–22)") and Northern Ireland as "countries" in provisions relating to taxation.
* The [Northern Ireland Act 1998](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Act_1998 "Northern Ireland Act 1998"), which repealed the Government of Ireland Act 1920, does not use any term to describe Northern Ireland.

#### Current legal terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Current legal terminology")]

The [Interpretation Act 1978](/wiki/Interpretation_Act_1978 "Interpretation Act 1978") provides statutory definitions of the terms "England", "Wales" and the "United Kingdom", but neither that Act nor any other current statute defines "Scotland" or "Northern Ireland". Use of the first three terms in other legislation is interpreted following the definitions in the 1978 Act. The definitions in the 1978 Act are listed below:

* "England" means, "subject to any alteration of boundaries under Part IV of the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"), the area consisting of the counties established by section 1 of that Act, Greater London and the [Isles of Scilly](/wiki/Isles_of_Scilly "Isles of Scilly")." This definition applies from 1 April 1974.
* "United Kingdom" means "Great Britain and Northern Ireland." This definition applies from 12 April 1927.
* "Wales" means the combined area of the 8 [Preserved counties of Wales](/wiki/Preserved_counties_of_Wales "Preserved counties of Wales") as outlined section 20 of the Local Government Act 1972, as originally enacted, but subject to any alteration made under section 73 of that Act (consequential alteration of boundary following alteration of watercourse). In 1996 these eight [new counties](/wiki/Preserved_counties_of_Wales "Preserved counties of Wales") were redistributed into the current [22 unitary authorities](/wiki/Wales#Local_government "Wales").

In Welsh law, "Wales" and "Cymru" are defined in the [Legislation (Wales) Act 2019](/wiki/Legislation_(Wales)_Act_2019 "Legislation (Wales) Act 2019") as "(a) the combined area of the counties and county boroughs in Wales (see Parts 1 and 2 of Schedule 4 to the Local Government Act 1972 (c. 70)), together with (b) the sea adjacent to Wales within the seaward limits of the territorial sea."

In the [Scotland Act 1998](/wiki/Scotland_Act_1998 "Scotland Act 1998") there is no delineation of Scotland, with the definition in section 126 simply providing that Scotland includes "so much of the internal waters and territorial sea of the United Kingdom as are adjacent to Scotland".[[28]](#cite_note-34)

The [Parliamentary Voting System and Constituencies Act 2011](/wiki/Parliamentary_Voting_System_and_Constituencies_Act_2011 "Parliamentary Voting System and Constituencies Act 2011") refers to England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland as "*parts*" of the United Kingdom in the following clause: "Each constituency shall be wholly in one of the four parts of the United Kingdom (England, Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland)."

### Other official usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Other official usage")]

The [Royal Fine Art Commission](/wiki/Royal_Fine_Art_Commission_(1840s) "Royal Fine Art Commission (1840s)")'s 1847 report on decorating the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster") referred to "the nationality of the component parts of the United Kingdom" being represented by their four respective patron saints.[[29]](#cite_note-35)

#### European Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: European Union")]

For the purposes of [NUTS 1](/wiki/NUTS_1_statistical_regions_of_the_United_Kingdom "NUTS 1 statistical regions of the United Kingdom") collection of statistical data in a format that is compatible with similar data collected in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (on behalf of [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat")), the United Kingdom was divided into twelve regions of approximately equal size.[[30]](#cite_note-36) Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland were regions in their own right while [England was divided into nine regions](/wiki/ITL_1_statistical_regions_of_England "ITL 1 statistical regions of England"). Following [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit"), the [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") uses [International Territorial Level](/wiki/International_Territorial_Level "International Territorial Level"), which is currently a mirror of the NUTS 1 system until the 2024 review.[[31]](#cite_note-37)

#### Current

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Current")]

The official term *rest of the UK* (RUK or rUK) is used in Scotland, for example in export statistics[[32]](#cite_note-38) and in legislating for [student funding](/wiki/Tuition_fees_in_the_United_Kingdom "Tuition fees in the United Kingdom").[[33]](#cite_note-39)

The alternative term *[Home Nations](/wiki/Home_Nations "Home Nations")* is sometimes used in sporting contexts and may include all of the island of [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland").

## Identity and nationality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Identity and nationality")]

Further information: [Demography of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Demography_of_the_United_Kingdom "Demography of the United Kingdom")

According to the [British Social Attitudes Survey](/wiki/British_Social_Attitudes_Survey "British Social Attitudes Survey"), there are broadly two interpretations of British identity, with ethnic and civic dimensions:

> The first group, which we term the ethnic dimension, contained the items about birthplace, ancestry, living in Britain, and sharing British customs and traditions. The second, or civic group, contained the items about feeling British, respecting laws and institutions, speaking English, and having British citizenship.[[34]](#cite_note-40)

Of the two perspectives of British identity, the civic definition has become the dominant idea and in this capacity, [Britishness](/wiki/Britishness "Britishness") is sometimes considered an institutional or overarching [state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") identity.[[35]](#cite_note-41)[[36]](#cite_note-42) This has been used to explain why first-, second- and third-generation immigrants are more likely to describe themselves as British, rather than English, Northern Irish, Scottish or Welsh, because it is an "institutional, inclusive" identity, that can be acquired through [naturalisation](/wiki/Naturalisation "Naturalisation") and [British nationality law](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law"); the vast majority of people in the United Kingdom who are from an ethnic minority feel British.[[37]](#cite_note-43) However, this attitude is more common in England than in Scotland or Wales; "white English people perceived themselves as English first and as British second, and most people from ethnic minority backgrounds perceived themselves as British, but none identified as English, a label they associated exclusively with white people".[[38]](#cite_note-44) Contrariwise, in Scotland and Wales "there was a much stronger
identification with each country than with Britain."[[39]](#cite_note-45)

The [Commission for Racial Equality](/wiki/Commission_for_Racial_Equality "Commission for Racial Equality") found that with respect to notions of [nationality](/wiki/Nationality "Nationality") in Britain, "the most basic, objective and uncontroversial conception of the British people is one that includes the English, the Scots and the Welsh".[[40]](#cite_note-CRE22-46) However, "English participants tended to think of themselves as indistinguishably English or British, while both Scottish and Welsh participants identified themselves much more readily as Scottish[[41]](#cite_note-47) or Welsh than as British".[[40]](#cite_note-CRE22-46) Some people opted "to combine both identities" as "they felt Scottish or Welsh, but held a [British passport](/wiki/British_passport "British passport") and were therefore British", whereas others saw themselves as exclusively Scottish or exclusively Welsh and "felt quite divorced from the British, whom they saw as the English".[[40]](#cite_note-CRE22-46) Commentators have described this latter viewpoint as "[nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism")", a rejection of British identity because some Scots and Welsh interpret it as "[cultural imperialism](/wiki/Cultural_imperialism "Cultural imperialism") imposed" upon the United Kingdom by "English ruling elites",[[42]](#cite_note-Ward2-3-48) or else a response to a historical misappropriation of equating the word "English" with "British",[[43]](#cite_note-49) which has "brought about a desire among Scots, Welsh and Irish to learn more about their heritage and distinguish themselves from the broader British identity".[[44]](#cite_note-50) The propensity for nationalistic feeling varies greatly across the UK, and can rise and fall over time.[[45]](#cite_note-51)

The 2011 census which asked about national identity found that responders in Great Britain predominantly chose English, Welsh and Scottish rather than British.[[46]](#cite_note-52) Research has suggested that most people in England and Wales tend to see themselves as British, but that in Wales and Scotland in particular, Scottishness or Welshness tends to receive more emphasis. The 2022 National Scotland census which asked about national identity in Scotland found that from the populations responders at 89% had predominantly chose the Scottish only identity at 65.5% of the population, the percentage of those identifying as British only increased to 13.9%, those identifying as Scottish and British had reduced to 8.2%.[[47]](#cite_note-53)

A poll conducted in Wales during spring 2019 found that 21% saw themselves as Welsh not British, 27% as more Welsh than British, 44% as equally Welsh and British whilst 7% saw themselves as either more or exclusively British.[[48]](#cite_note-54) A 2018 survey of 20,000 adults in England found that 80% identified strongly as English and 82% identified strongly as British, with the two identities appearing to be closely intertwined.[[49]](#cite_note-55)

The state-funded Northern Ireland Life and Times Survey,[[50]](#cite_note-56) part of a joint project between the [University of Ulster](/wiki/University_of_Ulster "University of Ulster") and [Queen's University Belfast](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_Belfast "Queen's University Belfast"), has addressed the issue of identity since it started polling in 1998. It reported that 37% of people identified as British, whilst 29% identified as Irish and 24% identified as Northern Irish. 3% opted to identify themselves as Ulster, whereas 7% stated 'other'. Of the two main religious groups, 68% of Protestants identified as British as did 6% of Catholics; 60% of Catholics identified as Irish as did 3% of Protestants. 21% of Protestants and 26% of Catholics identified as Northern Irish.[[51]](#cite_note-57)

For Northern Ireland, however, the results of the Life & Times Survey are not the whole story. The poll asks for a single preference, whereas many people easily identify as any combination of British and Irish, or British, Northern Irish and Irish, or Irish and Northern Irish. The 2014 Life & Times Survey addressed this to an extent by choosing two of the options from the identity question: British and Irish. It found that, while 28% of respondents stated they felt "British *not* Irish" and 26% felt "Irish *not* British", 39% of respondents felt some combination of both identities. Six percent chose 'other description'.[[52]](#cite_note-ark.ac.uk-58)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

The identity question is confounded further by identity with politics and religion, and particularly by a stance on the constitutional status of Northern Ireland. Again in 2014, the Life & Times Survey asked what respondents felt should be the "long term future for Northern Ireland". 66% of respondents felt the future should be as a part of the UK, with or without devolved government. 17% felt that Northern Ireland should unify with the Republic of Ireland. 50% of specifically Roman Catholics considered that the long-term future should be as part of the UK, with 32% opting for separation. 87% of respondents identifying as any Protestant denomination opted for remaining part of the UK, with only 4% opting for separation. Of those respondents who declared no religion, 62% opted for remaining part of the UK, with 9% opting for separation.[[52]](#cite_note-ark.ac.uk-58)

Following [devolution](/wiki/Devolution#United_Kingdom "Devolution") and the significant broadening of autonomous governance throughout the UK in the late 1990s, debate has taken place across the United Kingdom on the relative value of full [independence](/wiki/Independence "Independence"),[[53]](#cite_note-59) an option that was rejected[[54]](#cite_note-60) by the Scottish people in the [2014 Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum").

[Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") is administered as a [county of England](/wiki/County_of_England "County of England"), but the [Cornish people](/wiki/Cornish_people "Cornish people") are a recognised national minority, included under the terms of the [Framework Convention for the Protection of National Minorities](/wiki/Framework_Convention_for_the_Protection_of_National_Minorities "Framework Convention for the Protection of National Minorities") in 2014.[[55]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-61)[[56]](#cite_note-govcornish-62) In July 2025, [Cornwall Council](/wiki/Cornwall_Council "Cornwall Council") adopted the policy of supporting Cornwall's recognition as the UK's fifth nation alongside England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.[[57]](#cite_note-63) Within Cornwall, 13.8 per cent of the population associated themselves with a Cornish identity, either on its own or combined with other identities, according to the 2011 census. This data, however, was recorded without an available tick box for Cornish; so the percentage of the population within Cornwall associating with Cornish identity is likely higher.[[58]](#cite_note-64) In September 2025, Cornwall Council backed a public petition of 24,000 signatories calling for Cornish nationhood. Responding to the petition, the government said it did not plan to change Cornwall's constitutional status, instead favouring greater devolution to Cornwall within England.[[59]](#cite_note-65)

## Competitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Competitions")]

See also: [Home Nations](/wiki/Home_Nations "Home Nations")

Each of England, Northern Ireland, Scotland, and [Wales](/wiki/List_of_sports_governing_bodies_in_Wales "List of sports governing bodies in Wales") has separate [national governing bodies for sports](/wiki/Sport_governing_body "Sport governing body") and competes separately in many international sporting competitions.[[60]](#cite_note-66)[[61]](#cite_note-67)[[62]](#cite_note-68)[[63]](#cite_note-69) Each country of the United Kingdom has a [national football team](/wiki/List_of_men%27s_national_association_football_teams#UEFA_(Europe) "List of men's national association football teams"), and competes as a separate national team in the various disciplines in the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games").[[64]](#cite_note-worldpeoples-70) At the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), the United Kingdom is represented by the [Great Britain and Northern Ireland](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_Olympics "Great Britain at the Olympics") team, although athletes from Northern Ireland can choose to join the [Republic of Ireland's Olympic team](/wiki/Ireland_at_the_Olympics "Ireland at the Olympics").[[64]](#cite_note-worldpeoples-70)[[65]](#cite_note-71) In addition to Northern Ireland having its own national governing bodies for some sports such as [association football](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") and [netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball"), for others, such as [rugby union](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_union_team "Ireland national rugby union team") and [cricket](/wiki/Ireland_cricket_team "Ireland cricket team"), Northern Ireland participates with the Republic of Ireland in a joint [All-Ireland](/wiki/All-Ireland "All-Ireland") team. [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales_Cricket_Board "England and Wales Cricket Board") field a joint [cricket team](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team").

The United Kingdom participates in the [Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest "Eurovision Song Contest") as a single entity, though there have been calls for separate [Scottish](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Eurovision_Song_Contest "Scotland in the Eurovision Song Contest") and [Welsh](/wiki/Wales_in_the_Eurovision_Song_Contest "Wales in the Eurovision Song Contest") entrants. In 2017, Wales participated alone in the spin-off [Eurovision Choir](/wiki/Eurovision_Choir "Eurovision Choir"), followed by a separate entry for Scotland in 2019.[[66]](#cite_note-72) Wales also participated alone in the [Junior Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/Junior_Eurovision_Song_Contest "Junior Eurovision Song Contest") in 2018 and 2019.[[67]](#cite_note-73)

These names can be controversial, with the choice often revealing one's political preferences. ... some refer to Northern Ireland as a 'province'. That usage can arouse irritation particularly among nationalists, who claim the title 'province' should be properly reserved to the four historical provinces of Ireland-Ulster, Leinster, Munster, and Connacht. If I want to a label to apply to Northern Ireland I shall call it a 'region'. Unionists should find that title as acceptable as 'province': Northern Ireland appears as a region in the regional statistics of the United Kingdom published by the British government."
